content.assessment: Removing only the age-10 entry point targets the youngest and, per cross-disciplinary tracking evidence, potentially most inequality-generating selection stage, while leaving the age-12 and age-14 tracks intact. This partial approach reduces the selection funnel from three to two stages but risks displacement rather than reduction of sorting, since the age-12 entry point could absorb the demand previously channeled through age-10 selection, intensifying competition there. Given that school-type segregation and SES-based stratification are described as major structural channels operating across the tracking system, a partial fix leaves substantial segregation-generating capacity in place at the remaining age-12 and age-14 points.
opportunities: Could serve as an initial step that reduces the most extreme early-selection harms while preserving political feasibility for further incremental reform
strengths: Targets removal of the earliest selection point, which cross-national tracking evidence associates with heightened inequality risk given younger children's outcomes being less reflective of stable ability signals
Reduces overall selection-funnel exposure from three stages to two, a partial reduction in relocatable sorting opportunities
threats: Displaced demand concentrating at age-12 could produce equally or more intense sorting effects at that remaining entry point, undermining the equity rationale for removing only the youngest track
The structural link between tracking and Hungary's severe socioeconomic achievement gradient may remain substantially intact if two of three selection points persist
weaknesses: Segregation mechanisms tied to school-type stratification and SES may persist largely unchanged if the retained 8-year and 4-year tracks continue similar selection dynamics
Risk of demand displacement toward the age-12 entry point could intensify competition and sorting there without net reduction in segregation
